title: Implementing Interfaces
description: Forcing classes to follow the rules.

slides.title: Strict Contracts 📜
slides.content:
When designing huge applications, architects often write an Interface outlining all the methods a Class *must* possess to work within the system.

A Class can cleanly promise to fulfill those requirements by `implementing` the Interface.

slides.title: The 'implements' Keyword
slides.content: We force the class to conform using the `implements` keyword.

quizData.question: What happens if a Class implements an Interface but you completely forget to write out the method the Interface demands?

options.text: It defaults to basically doing nothing.
TypeScript refuses to compile, throwing an error immediately stating the class fundamentally broke the contract.
TypeScript automatically generates a blank method for you.

quizData.explanation: TypeScript acts as strict enforcement. If you promise to implement a Logger, you must mathematically write the `log()` function.

slides.title: Multiple Interfaces 🦸
slides.content:
Unlike Class Inheritance (where a class can only inherit from *one* parent class), a Class can implement multiple Interfaces at the absolute same time!

e.g., `class SmartTV implements Displayable, InternetConnected { ... }`

slides.title: Design Philosophy 🏗️
slides.content: Interfaces enforce the *Shape* of the class (the what), but the Class writes the actual *Logic* (the how). This provides highly modular, testable code architectures!
